Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Jaak Panksepp

1943 – 2017

Affective neuroscience and the primal emotional systems of the brain.

A comprehensive philosophy of mind can be extracted from Panksepp, but it must not be extracted as though he were a speculative metaphysician first and a neuroscientist second. His philosophy of mind is implicit in his affective neuroscience. Its central claim is that mind begins as felt biological value: not as language, not as detached cognition, not as cortical representation, and not as a private immaterial substance, but as the organism’s affectively charged, action-oriented way of being alive. In Panksepp’s mature vocabulary, the deepest form of mind is an anoetic affective consciousness: experience without reflective knowing, a primal “feeling of life” generated by ancient subcortical systems and later elaborated by learning, memory, narrative, culture, moral reflection, and religion. Panksepp’s “soul,” when he uses that word, is best understood as this embodied, affective, organismic core of subjectivity—the core SELF or, with his own poetic license, the “animalian soul.”

This report treats his theology-adjacent comments as integral rather than decorative. Panksepp’s references to the soul, prayer, religion, Descartes, God, and Thandeka’s affect theology are not accidental eruptions of metaphor in an otherwise purely technical project. They mark one of the deepest consequences of his work: the old Western identification of soul with disembodied rationality is, for him, scientifically and ethically inadequate. If the root of mind is affective, embodied, subcortical, and shared with other mammals, then the human soul cannot be treated as a purely rational, uniquely human, emotionless “divine spark” set over against animal life.

1. The basic architecture: BrainMind, MindBrain, and dual-aspect affective monism

Panksepp’s metaphysical position is not Cartesian dualism, behaviorism, or eliminative materialism. He repeatedly describes his position as a form of dual-aspect monism, explicitly “Spinozan” in some formulations: the same living neural processes can be described from the side of brain dynamics and from the side of lived affective experience. His term BrainMind/MindBrain is meant to prevent us from severing neural events from mental events, while still allowing different explanatory directions: “BrainMind” for bottom-up, neural-to-experiential processes, and “MindBrain” for top-down, cognitive-cultural modulation of lower affective systems.

This is a monism of living process, not a reductive identity theory in the crude sense. Panksepp does not say that feelings are “nothing but” third-person neural firings, as though the first-person aspect were an illusion. Rather, he claims that emotional feelings are the experiential aspect of large-scale neurodynamics in evolved emotional action systems. In the 2005 “Affective consciousness” article, he states that the bedrock of emotional feelings lies in mammalian emotional action apparatuses and that core emotional feelings may reflect “neurodynamic attractor landscapes” of trans-diencephalic, limbic emotional action systems.

The resulting ontology is best called affective naturalism. The world contains living bodies whose neural organization produces, or perhaps more carefully instantiates, felt values. These felt values are not decorations added to cognition; they are the primitive basis on which cognition becomes meaningful. Panksepp’s 2011 article on mammalian emotional circuits describes primal affects as intrinsic brain value systems that tell animals how they are faring in survival: positive affects mark viable “comfort zones,” while negative affects signal conditions that may impair survival.

2. Consciousness: Panksepp’s hierarchy of anoetic, noetic, and autonoetic mind

Panksepp’s most important philosophical move is to distinguish consciousness from reflective consciousness. If consciousness means internal experience, then primary-process affect is not unconscious. It is conscious in an anoetic sense: it feels, but it does not yet know that it feels. It lacks explicit self-reflection, autobiographical narrative, and verbal report, but it is not thereby non-conscious. Panksepp calls this “affective consciousness,” and he says these anoetic circuits can generate powerful rewards and punishments when activated.

His layered model can be summarized as follows. Primary-process consciousness consists of raw affective, sensory, homeostatic, and emotional feelings rooted in ancient subcortical systems. Secondary processes include learning, conditioning, memory, and the mapping of affect onto objects, places, and situations. Tertiary processes include reflective cognition, language, narrative selfhood, moral deliberation, and explicit awareness of awareness. This three-level structure is repeated across Panksepp’s work and in later Pankseppian summaries: primary affects arise from subcortical homologous systems, secondary processes concern learning and memory, and tertiary processes are especially dependent on neocortical elaboration.

The philosophical consequence is severe: higher cognition is not the birthplace of consciousness. Cognition elaborates consciousness, interprets it, narrates it, regulates it, and sometimes distorts it. But on Panksepp’s view, the primal fact that “something matters” to the organism is already present at the affective level. This is why he can say that many higher mental abilities may be unconscious, while affective foundations are intensely experienced and often difficult to verbalize.

A Pankseppian philosophy of consciousness is therefore bottom-up and affect-first. It reverses theories that make consciousness essentially linguistic, cortical, or higher-order. It does not deny higher-order consciousness; it denies that higher-order consciousness is the only real consciousness. This puts Panksepp in direct tension with higher-order and constructionist theories of emotional consciousness, such as LeDoux and Brown’s proposal that conscious emotional experience depends on a general cortical network rather than directly on subcortical emotional circuits.

3. The seven primary affective systems as primitive forms of mindedness

Panksepp’s famous seven primary systems are not merely a taxonomy of emotions. They are, philosophically, primitive modes of world-disclosure: ways in which the organism encounters the world as inviting, threatening, frustrating, bonding, erotic, bereaved, or playable. In his own mature list, the seven are SEEKING, RAGE, FEAR, LUST, CARE, PANIC/GRIEF, and PLAY. They are capitalized because Panksepp means neural-affective systems, not ordinary folk-psychological labels.

SEEKING is perhaps the most metaphysically important. It is not simple pleasure; it is exploratory, appetitive, world-opening intentionality. It gives the organism the sense that there is something to find, pursue, solve, obtain, or discover. FEAR discloses danger and organizes avoidance, freezing, and escape. RAGE discloses obstruction or invasion and mobilizes defensive attack. PANIC/GRIEF discloses separation and loss, especially in attachment bonds. CARE discloses the vulnerable other as to-be-nurtured. LUST discloses erotic-reproductive possibility. PLAY discloses others and the world as safe enough for joyful, flexible, social experimentation.

The point is not that these seven systems exhaust all human emotion. Panksepp’s view is that they are primary-process affective generators on which more complex emotions are built. Jealousy, shame, guilt, religious longing, political loyalty, moral outrage, aesthetic awe, and spiritual consolation are not simple one-to-one expressions of a single primary circuit. They are tertiary and culturally elaborated formations built from primary affects, secondary learning histories, and symbolic cognition. Panksepp explicitly allows tertiary processes to generate “infinite variations” of complex affective life, while insisting that those variations remain grounded in ancestral affective foundations.

This yields a philosophy of mind in which affect is intentional without first being conceptual. A frightened animal does not need a proposition such as “this object is dangerous” in order for the world to be disclosed as threatening. A separated infant does not need a concept of attachment in order to suffer separation distress. A playing juvenile mammal does not need a theory of sociality in order to experience playful joy. For Panksepp, mindedness begins in affective action-readiness, not in detached representation.

4. Subcortical primacy and the critique of cortical imperialism

Panksepp’s strongest empirical-philosophical thesis is that the neocortex is not necessary for primary affective consciousness. He argues that primary emotional mechanisms are concentrated in low, medial, evolutionarily ancient brain regions: midbrain, diencephalon, basal ganglia, hypothalamic and periaqueductal systems, and related subcortical networks. In his cross-species work, he stresses that stimulation of ancient subcortical regions elicits coherent emotional action patterns, that animals will work to obtain or avoid such stimulation, and that stimulation of homologous sites in humans yields corresponding affective reports.

This does not make the cortex irrelevant. The cortex elaborates, inhibits, symbolizes, contextualizes, and regulates affect. It permits autobiographical consciousness, linguistic description, complex planning, cultural learning, and many moral emotions. But the cortex is not, on Panksepp’s view, the original source of raw feeling. Davis and Montag’s Pankseppian review makes this explicit: primary emotional and homeostatic affects do not require the neocortex, even though neocortical processes are crucial for later reflective and interpretive forms of emotion.

This has an important philosophical implication: mind is phylogenetically deeper than human rationality. Mammalian consciousness is not a defective version of human language-mediated consciousness. Rather, human reflective consciousness is an elaboration of older affective consciousness. Panksepp’s work thus supports a continuity model: the differences between human and nonhuman minds are profound, especially at tertiary cognitive levels, but the foundations of affective sentience are shared.

5. Method: how Panksepp thinks animal consciousness can be studied

Panksepp does not claim that animal feelings can be observed directly. He repeatedly acknowledges that there are no “mindscopes.” His method is not naïve anthropomorphism but triangulation: homologous brain circuits, controlled brain stimulation and pharmacology, emotional behavior, learning effects, reward and aversion, vocalizations, lesion evidence, and human reports from homologous neural stimulation. He argues that although definitive proof is unavailable, the weight of convergent evidence supports affective experience in other mammals.

The crucial methodological point is that behavior alone is insufficient, but brain-behavior-experience triangulation is powerful. If electrical stimulation of a subcortical site produces coherent emotional behavior, if animals learn to approach or avoid that stimulation, if homologous stimulation in humans produces corresponding affective reports, and if the relevant circuits are evolutionarily conserved, then the inference to animal feeling becomes scientifically reasonable. Panksepp calls this kind of cross-species affective inference more valid at primary subcortical levels than at tertiary cognitive levels.

This is also why he objects to treating “reinforcement” as a merely behavioral black box. For Panksepp, reinforcement is not a mysterious external law of learning; it is the behavioral trace of felt reward and punishment systems. A learning theory that refuses to ask whether rewards and punishments are experienced is, from his standpoint, methodologically impoverished.

6. The core SELF and the neurobiology of the soul

The most explicit soul-language appears in The Archaeology of Mind, where chapter 11 is titled “Toward a Neurobiology of the Soul: The Core SELF and Genesis of Primary-Process Feelings.” Panksepp and Biven describe the core SELF, with “poetic license,” as an “animalian soul”: a Simple Ego-type Life Form, a coherent center of gravity for internal visceral-affective and external sensorimotor representations.

This core SELF is not the autobiographical ego. It is not the social persona, not the Cartesian thinking subject, not the Freudian ego in its full metapsychological complexity, and not a linguistic narrative. It is the organism’s primitive center of felt aliveness: the affective “me-here-now” through which bodily needs, emotional urges, and action-readiness become unified. Panksepp’s inversion of Descartes is decisive: before “I think, therefore I am,” there is “I feel, therefore I am.”

This core SELF is also where Panksepp most nearly addresses the hard problem. He does not pretend to have solved it. In The Archaeology of Mind, he concedes that no one knows how material brain processes beget mind or “me,” but he argues that testable hypotheses should begin with ancient medial brainstem and subcortical systems where primary affective feelings arise. The philosophical humility matters: Panksepp offers a research program for locating and functionally characterizing the affective sources of subjectivity, not a complete metaphysical derivation of experience from matter.

The “soul” in this framework is therefore naturalized but not trivialized. It is not reduced to a lifeless mechanism. It is relocated in the living, feeling, affectively organized body. This is a profound anti-dualist move: what earlier traditions called soul may have its deepest biological correlate in the ancient affective systems that generate suffering, longing, joy, attachment, play, erotic drive, fear, and care.

7. Panksepp’s implicit phenomenology: value, not representation, is first

Panksepp’s philosophy of consciousness is closer to a phenomenology of value than to a theory of inner pictures. The first function of consciousness is not to mirror the world neutrally but to disclose the world as good, bad, dangerous, inviting, frustrating, lonely, or safe. Affects are not merely internal colors added to cognition; they are the organism’s felt registration of biological meaning.

Spanish-language reception has seen this point clearly. Daniel A. León’s article “Afectividad y conciencia: la experiencia subjetiva de los valores biológicos” frames Pankseppian affective neuroscience as a model for the origin and function of conscious experience, with subjectivity understood as the central attribute of consciousness and affective processes allowing organisms to feel the biological values of action in relation to environmental demands.

This is one of the strongest places to extract a systematic philosophy. For Panksepp, consciousness is originally evaluative, embodied, and practical. The mind does not first represent a world and then add value to it. Rather, the organism’s world is first opened through value-laden action systems. Representation, concept, judgment, and narrative are later achievements built upon this affective disclosure.

8. Animal consciousness and ethics

Panksepp’s work has direct ethical implications. If primary affective consciousness is shared across mammals, then animal suffering and animal joy are not sentimental projections. They are probable features of homologous affective systems. Panksepp’s 2011 work states that at least all other mammals experience emotional arousals, though he remains cautious about the precise qualitative nature of those experiences.

This underwrites an ethical critique of Cartesian animal-machine thinking. In The Archaeology of Mind, Panksepp and Biven discuss the historical role of Descartes and the idea of a uniquely human divine spark, arguing that the denial of animal subjectivity contributed to inhumane experimentation. Panksepp’s affective neuroscience reverses this: shared subcortical affective systems make animals ethically considerable as feeling beings, not merely behaving organisms.

The same logic extends, controversially, to human disorders of consciousness. In the 2007 article on persistent vegetative state, Panksepp and coauthors argue that raw affective feelings may survive forms of brain damage that abolish reflective awareness, raising ethical questions about pain, thirst, suffering, and withdrawal of life support. Whether one accepts every conclusion or not, the philosophical point is consistent: lack of cognitive report does not entail lack of affective consciousness.

9. Religion, prayer, and the affective roots of theological life

Panksepp’s comments on religion are not a full theology, but they are philosophically important. He interprets some religious behaviors through the SEEKING system and through what he calls adjunctive behavior: when organisms are highly aroused to act but cannot effectively change the situation, ritualized action can reduce distress or create a felt sense of agency. He explicitly discusses rain dances and prayer in this light, asking whether prayer can give human beings the feeling that they can magically alter fate in conditions of helplessness.

This could sound reductive, but Panksepp’s broader account is more nuanced. He also connects religion to attachment, nurturance, grief, social bonding, and the human search for a higher good. In The Archaeology of Mind, religion is associated not only with SEEKING but with the affective need for care, community, protection against grief, and longing for a nurturing higher power. His discussion of isolated infant monkeys and prayer-like postures is speculative, but its purpose is not mockery; it is to understand religious longing as continuous with mammalian attachment systems.

The theological significance is substantial. Panksepp’s work suggests that religion is not primarily an intellectual assent to propositions, nor merely a social construction imposed on neutral bodies. Religious life emerges from the affective architecture of vulnerability, loss, hope, attachment, exploration, awe, and communal repair. That does not prove or disprove theological truth claims. It changes the anthropology within which theology must work.

This is where Thandeka’s affect theology becomes relevant. Panksepp commended Thandeka’s work as providing a possible “universal substrate for nondenominational religious experiences,” and contemporary affect theology has explicitly taken Panksepp’s neuroscience as a challenge to the idea that the human soul or spirit is a strictly rational, disembodied, emotionless entity.

A Pankseppian theology of mind would therefore be neither crude neuro-reductionism nor supernatural dualism. It would treat religious experience as affectively embodied and biologically grounded while leaving open the interpretive question of whether those affective depths are merely natural, sacramental, creaturely, revelatory, or some combination of these. The main theological correction is this: any doctrine of soul that ignores fear, grief, care, play, longing, attachment, and joy is not describing the living human soul as affective neuroscience reveals it.

10. Panksepp against rationalist anthropology

Panksepp’s view is a sustained critique of rationalist anthropologies that define humans primarily by abstract cognition. He does not deny rationality; he denies its primacy. Reason is developmentally, evolutionarily, and motivationally dependent on affective systems. Portuguese philosophical reception makes this explicit: in discussing Panksepp’s contribution to decision-making, ethics, and epistemology, Luís Ferreira Almada argues that nothing still justifies the old philosophical thesis that reason is prior or primary relative to emotions, and that affective neuroscience reframes moral reasoning by showing how emotions provide natural internal values for complex choices.

This has implications for moral psychology. Moral judgment is not pure calculation later colored by emotion. Moral life is built from affective valuations—care, harm, anger, fear, loyalty, shame, grief, hope—organized by learning, culture, and reflection. Recent biblical scholarship has begun applying Panksepp this way: a 2025 study of 2 Corinthians 7:5–16 classifies Pauline emotions through Pankseppian categories such as FEAR/anxiety, GRIEF/separation distress, CARE/nurturing, and RAGE/anger, while also insisting that nativist and socio-cultural approaches should be complementary rather than mutually exclusive.

So, in philosophy-of-mind terms, Panksepp is not simply “emotion over reason.” He is arguing for a stratified integration: affective value systems at the base, learning and memory in the middle, and reflective cognition at the top, with continuous bottom-up and top-down interaction. Italian reception states this clearly, describing Panksepp’s monistic view of the human Mind/Brain as stratified: primary affective processes provide the phylogenetic and ontogenetic condition for secondary emotional learning and tertiary reflection on experience.

11. Multilingual reception: what non-English sources add

The non-English material I found did not overturn the Anglo-American reading of Panksepp, but it sharpened several emphases. Spanish sources foreground the relation between affect and consciousness as a problem of biological value. León’s Spanish article is especially philosophical: it treats Pankseppian affective neuroscience as a current conceptual framework for the nature of conscious experience and explicitly links affective subjectivity to the organism’s feeling of biological values. A Spanish neuroscience site also emphasizes the subcortical roots of primary emotions, the non-necessity of neocortex for primary affect, and the distinction between primary, secondary, and tertiary processes.

French reception appears more clinical and neuropsychoanalytic. One French essay notes that Panksepp was important for neuropsychoanalysis, integrating primary, secondary, and tertiary processes and offering clinicians a biologically grounded emotional system. A French neuroscience blog presents his work as a “rehabilitation” of subcortical structures and places his three levels—primary survival value and emotion, secondary learning and memory, tertiary cortex and culture—in continuity with but also beyond MacLean’s triune-brain framework.

Italian reception is particularly strong on psychoanalysis, personality, and the layered Mind/Brain. Italian psychoanalytic commentary emphasizes the monistic and stratified character of Panksepp’s account, while Italian clinical and popular-scientific sources stress that his seven affective systems are ancient, subcortical, neurochemical, and shared with other mammals. Some Italian discussion also highlights circular causation: cognitive top-down regulation can modify primary affective processes, but it does not abolish their foundational role.

Portuguese reception contributes a useful ethical-philosophical accent. Almada’s article treats Pankseppian affective neuroscience as relevant to decision-making, moral reasoning, action, normativity, ethics, and epistemology. Another Portuguese-language chapter distinguishes the behavioral, physiological, and affective aspects of emotion, defining affect as the lived or conscious experience of the emotional state, and notes that many emotion neuroscientists remain behavioristically reluctant to investigate emotional affects in other mammals.

German web material I found was less philosophically extensive, but it confirms the mainstream scientific reception: Panksepp is presented as a major researcher of the neural systems that mediate emotions, and emotion is described as involving experiential, physiological, behavioral, and action-readiness components rooted partly in evolutionarily old limbic systems.

12. Criticisms and unresolved problems

The first unresolved problem is the hard problem itself. Panksepp gives a powerful account of where and how affective consciousness is generated, how it functions, and how it can be studied comparatively. But he does not fully explain why those neural dynamics should have a first-person feel at all. He is candid about this: The Archaeology of Mind says no one knows how material processes beget mind or “me.” His contribution is to relocate the explanatory gap to ancient affective action systems rather than to language, cortex, or abstract cognition.

The second problem is cortical contribution. Panksepp sometimes writes polemically against cortical theories of emotion, but a balanced Pankseppian philosophy must preserve bidirectional causation. Cortex does not create primary affect, but it can reshape affect profoundly through attention, expectation, memory, regulation, narrative, and culture. Davis and Montag’s review explicitly suggests that Pankseppian basic-emotion theory and constructivist theories may be addressing different levels of control: subcortical primary processes versus neocortical emotional concepts.

The third problem is system individuation. Are there exactly seven primary systems? Are they as discrete as Panksepp sometimes suggests? How far beyond mammals do they extend? Panksepp’s 2011 review says all vertebrates appear to have some capacity for primal affective feelings, but his strongest evidence is mammalian and especially rodent-based. A cautious reconstruction should treat the seven systems as a robust but revisable map of affective organization, not as a final metaphysical periodic table of emotion.

The fourth problem is anthropomorphism. Panksepp’s answer is that anthropomorphism is dangerous at the wrong level and necessary at the right one. We should not project human tertiary concepts onto animals; but we may infer primary affective experience where homologous circuits, behavior, stimulation, learning, and human reports converge. This is better described as disciplined evolutionary inference than sentimental projection.

The fifth problem is theology. Affective neuroscience can describe the biological substrates and affective dynamics of prayer, longing, grief, consolation, and communal worship. It cannot by itself decide whether God exists, whether prayer is answered, whether grace is real, or whether religious experience has revelatory content. Theologically, Panksepp is most useful as an anthropology of affective creatureliness, not as a substitute for metaphysics, doctrine, or spiritual discernment.

13. Extracted Pankseppian philosophy of mind: systematic theses

A rigorous reconstruction of Panksepp’s philosophy of mind would include at least these theses.

First, mind is affectively rooted. The earliest and most basic form of mind is not propositional thought but felt valence: attraction, aversion, fear, pain, longing, comfort, play, care, and exploratory drive.

Second, consciousness is graded and layered. Anoetic affective consciousness precedes noetic object-consciousness and autonoetic autobiographical self-consciousness. Reflective self-awareness is a later achievement, not the criterion for all consciousness.

Third, the core self is embodied and subcortical before it is narrative and cortical. The primal “I” is not originally the thinking ego but the feeling organism. The core SELF or “animalian soul” is a center of affective-motor coherence.

Fourth, neural and mental descriptions are two aspects of one living process. This is Panksepp’s dual-aspect monism: feelings are not ghostly additions to brain processes, but neither are they eliminable fictions. They are the experiential aspect of organized living neurodynamics.

Fifth, affect is biological value made conscious. Feelings tell organisms how they are doing in relation to survival, attachment, exploration, threat, bodily need, and social possibility. This is why emotion is central to learning, decision-making, and moral life.

Sixth, cognition is affectively scaffolded. Tertiary thought, language, culture, and morality depend on lower affective systems, even when they regulate or oppose them. The highest mind is not independent of the oldest mind.

Seventh, animal consciousness is scientifically accessible by convergent evidence. We cannot introspect animal minds, but we can infer affective experience from homologous circuits, causal stimulation, reward and aversion, vocalizations, lesions, pharmacology, and human reports from homologous systems.

Eighth, ethics begins with sentience, not rational autonomy alone. If other mammals possess primary affective consciousness, then their suffering, fear, play, attachment, and distress matter morally. This also complicates medical ethics in humans who lack reflective report but may retain affective experience.

Ninth, the soul is affective before it is intellectual. Panksepp’s “neurobiology of the soul” does not abolish the soul but relocates it in the embodied affective core of life. The soul, in this naturalized sense, is the deep feeling organization of the organism.

Tenth, religion is an affective-symbolic elaboration of mammalian vulnerability and longing. Prayer, worship, religious symbols, and spiritual community are built from SEEKING, CARE, PANIC/GRIEF, attachment, helplessness, hope, and the need for a higher good. This does not settle theological truth, but it gives theology a biologically serious account of the feeling soul.

Conclusion

The philosophy of mind implicit in Panksepp is a subcortical, affective, evolutionary, embodied, dual-aspect monism. Its first principle is not “I think,” but “I feel.” Its basic subject is not the rational adult human, but the living organism whose world is disclosed through felt biological value. Its theory of consciousness is graded: raw affective experience comes first; reflective cognition comes later. Its theory of the self is layered: the core SELF or “animalian soul” precedes the autobiographical ego. Its ethics extends concern to animals and to cognitively impaired humans because feeling may survive where report and reflection fail. Its theology is not doctrinal, but it is theologically consequential: it challenges disembodied rationalist accounts of soul and invites a theology of affective creatureliness, attachment, grief, care, play, longing, and embodied aliveness.

Panksepp does not solve every metaphysical problem. He does not close the explanatory gap completely, nor does he settle debates about the cortical construction of emotion, the exact number of primary systems, or the truth of religious claims. But he gives one of the strongest modern scientific bases for a philosophy of mind in which consciousness is not an intellectual luxury added late to animal life. Consciousness begins, rather, in the ancient affective depths where life first becomes meaningful to itself.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
